sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Mr. James, a 65-year-old male, presents with a 5-month history of constant pain in his left shoulder and elbow, which began after a fall 5 months ago while walking up icy stairs.
He reports that the pain is present all the time and worsens with lifting and physical activity.
He has been trying to continue his exercise routine, but has had to limit his weightlifting to 15-20 pounds.
He denies numbness or tingling in his arm.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's left shoulder and elbow are tender to palpation.
There is no warmth, erythema, or deformity.
The patient is positive for the Neer's test and the kiss test.
Proximal and distal upper extremity strength is normal.
Sensation in the median, radial, and ulnar nerves is intact, and abduction to 90 degrees is normal.
The empty can test is also normal.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has impingement syndrome of the left shoulder.
Problem list: Shoulder impingement syndrome; suspected wrist injury.
Plan:
I ordered a referral for the patient to see Dr. Martha Rivera for physical therapy, 2 days a week.
I recommended that the patient continue to ice their shoulder and rest as much as possible.
The patient will be contacted within the next day or two to schedule physical therapy appointments.
I advised the patient to contact the office if their symptoms worsen or do not improve with physical therapy.
Follow-up: The patient will be contacted within the next day or two to schedule physical therapy appointments.
